questions.scenario: A Jenkins controller has been changed through the UI for two years. Nobody can reproduce its plugin set, security realm, agent definitions, or tool paths on a clean machine. Which first redesign creates a reviewable controller?

options.text: Continue approved UI changes and export only the top-level plugin list after each maintenance window.
Bake the current `JENKINS_HOME` and plugin directory into a reusable controller image, then externalize only credentials.
Build a controller image from a versioned plugin list and apply versioned JCasC YAML with secrets supplied at runtime.
Version only `jenkins.yaml` and rebuild the plugin set from each plugin's latest compatible release.

questions.explanation: A versioned image and JCasC file make the intended plugin set and controller configuration reviewable before production, while runtime secret sources keep values out of Git. Exporting only top-level plugins after UI changes misses dependency resolution and preserves change-after-the-fact drift. Baking live state into the image mixes durable state with software, and resolving latest compatible plugins does not reproduce a known set. See [Jenkins Configuration as Code](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/managing/casc/).

questions.scenario: After a Git plugin update, Jenkins boots and the UI looks normal, but every checkout fails with NoSuchMethodError in git-client. What does this evidence suggest?

options.text: A plugin API mismatch; stage a compatible plugin set or roll back.
The Git plugin loaded correctly, but the agent's Git CLI is older than the controller expects.
The controller restored an older `JENKINS_HOME` whose plugin metadata does not match the running image.
The affected agents are using a stale remoting runtime that cannot execute the updated checkout step.

questions.explanation: `NoSuchMethodError` means loaded code called a method absent from the loaded dependency version, which fits a Git and git-client plugin API mismatch after the update. A stale Git CLI or remoting runtime produces different execution failures. An older state directory may explain how versions drifted, but the immediate evidence still requires a compatible plugin set or rollback. See [Jenkins plugin management](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/managing/plugins/).

questions.scenario: Summit Retail is preparing to rebuild its Jenkins controller from versioned configuration. Its reviewed `jenkins.yaml` defines executors, authorization, Maven tools, and location settings, but it also contains a production password in plain text. The platform team must keep the controller shape reproducible without storing the secret in Git. Which change satisfies both requirements?

options.text: Keep credential identifiers in JCasC but create their values manually after every controller rebuild.
Encrypt the password with a controller-specific key and commit both the ciphertext and key reference.
Store the production password in an unversioned JCasC file on the controller disk and restore it manually after failures.
Keep the credential shape in YAML but reference a runtime environment or external secret source for the value.

questions.explanation: JCasC should describe controller shape while secret values come from a protected runtime environment or external secret source. Manual creation and controller-local unversioned files make recovery depend on operator memory and local disk. Controller-specific encryption can be useful at rest, but committing or tightly coupling its key material does not provide the required secret boundary. See [Jenkins Configuration as Code](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/managing/casc/).

questions.scenario: A reviewed JCasC change updates the system message and a role assignment. No plugin binaries, Jenkins core, Java version, or JVM flags changed. After staging validation, what is the smallest production action that can apply it?

options.text: Restart the tested controller image so the YAML is applied from process startup.
Reload the existing JCasC configuration and verify the affected behavior.
Apply the role assignment through the UI, then export the resulting YAML back to source control.
Replace the production controller with the staged controller instance and attach production state.

questions.explanation: Many YAML-only settings can be reapplied through JCasC reload, making it the smallest action for this reviewed change. A restart would also load the YAML but introduces unnecessary process disruption. UI mutation recreates drift, while replacing the controller and attaching production state expands the change far beyond the two settings. See [Jenkins Configuration as Code](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/managing/casc/).

questions.scenario: The controller image changes Jenkins LTS, two plugin versions, Java, and Xmx. An operator proposes a JCasC reload to avoid downtime. Which action is actually required?

options.text: Reload JCasC for controller settings, then hot-copy the new plugin archives into the running plugin directory.
Restart the current image after replacing only the two plugin archives, leaving Jenkins core, Java, and JVM flags unchanged.
Restart into the tested image because these components load only at process startup.
Roll out the tested image but use a JCasC reload hook instead of restarting the Jenkins process.

questions.explanation: JCasC configures Jenkins objects; it does not replace the running JVM, core application, classloader, or startup flags. Those changes require a new process from the tested image. See [Jenkins managing plugins](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/managing/plugins/).

questions.scenario: A plugin security advisory requires an accelerated update. The team can build a candidate image within an hour. Which evidence package should accompany production promotion?

options.text: Plugin diff, dependency output, JCasC check, staging jobs, and a rollback note.
The advisory, plugin diff, and a successful controller login using the candidate image.
Plugin dependency resolution and JCasC validation, with production used for the first representative job.
Representative staging jobs and a rollback image, but no check of state compatibility with that older image.

questions.explanation: An emergency shortens the timeline, not the evidence required. The plugin diff and dependency result establish the candidate set, JCasC validation checks controller shape, representative staging jobs exercise real integrations, and the rollback note addresses state compatibility. Login alone misses checkout and agents. Dependency checks without staging defer runtime risk to production, while an unvalidated rollback image may not read migrated state. See [Jenkins plugin management](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/managing/plugins/).

questions.scenario: The controller has accumulated manual plugin changes and its last emergency upgrade lacked rollback evidence. Which practices support a sustainable Jenkins upgrade cadence?

options.text: Allow emergency production plugin changes, then rebuild the versioned image from the live controller after stabilization.
Build and boot a staging controller from the same versioned inputs intended for production.
Keep the previous image as the rollback target without testing whether plugin migrations changed `JENKINS_HOME`.
Run representative pipelines that exercise agents, source checkout, credentials, and deployment integrations.
Write rollback notes and account for JENKINS_HOME compatibility before the production window.

questions.explanation: A staging controller, representative jobs, and explicit state-aware rollback planning test the real upgrade. Direct production mutation creates drift, and plugin migrations can make a previous image incompatible with current state. See [Jenkins plugin management](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/managing/plugins/) and [Configuration as Code](https://www.jenkins.io/doc/book/managing/casc/).
